title: CVE, CT & Stabilization – Somalia
starter_prompts: What are the CVE entry points in al-Shabaab-controlled or -influenced areas?
How do I engage communities in recently liberated territories?
What are the risks of being associated with military actors in stabilization work?
How does al-Shabaab's taxation system affect our operations?
What does a defector reintegration pathway look like in practice?

situation_overview: Al-Shabaab is a designated terrorist organization that controls significant territory across South Central Somalia and operates as a shadow government — collecting over $150 million annually through taxation and extortion, adjudicating disputes, delivering services, and enforcing compliance — often more effectively than the Federal Government of Somalia (FGS). The FGS launched a major offensive in August 2022 alongside clan militias (Ma'awisley), recapturing over 215 locations and approximately 65,000 square kilometers by April 2023. However, the offensive stalled through 2024, and by early 2025 al-Shabaab launched a major counteroffensive in Middle Shabelle and beyond. By July 2025, al-Shabaab had recaptured most of Middle Shabelle (including Adan Yabaal in April, and Sabiid and Anole in July), was operating within 50km of Mogadishu, and had formed a strategic triangle (Moqokori–Tardo–Buq-Aqable) enabling encirclement of government supply lines. Uganda withdrew AUSSOM forces after two suicide vehicle bomb attacks killed 30+ troops. Al-Shabaab conducted over 160 attacks against Mogadishu civilians in the 2024–2025 period, including the Beach View Hotel (287 casualties, August 2024) and SYL Hotel (34 casualties, March 2024). A separate and distinct threat — Islamic State-Somalia — operates in Puntland's Bari region, where it serves as a global IS financing hub; Puntland's Operation Hilaac (launched January 2025) is the primary counter, with US and UAE airstrike support. The US escalated airstrikes dramatically in 2025: 38 strikes between February 1 and June 10 (22 against IS-Somalia, 16 against al-Shabaab), nearly 4x the total for all of 2024. Political fragmentation is the central obstacle: Puntland has boycotted the NCC since January 2023, Jubaland-FGS tensions produced armed clashes in late 2024, and 145 police officers 'disappeared' during al-Shabaab's 2025 offensive (some suspected defectors). AUSSOM, which replaced ATMIS in January 2025, is authorized at 11,911 personnel across four sectors but faces a severe funding crisis with less than 20% of costs covered. Field operators work in a complex environment where al-Shabaab controls access, taxes humanitarian operations, and CT financing laws create serious legal risk for engagement. CVE and stabilization work must be differentiated by territorial control zone: al-Shabaab permanent control, semi-permanent presence, contested, and FGS-controlled.

armed_groups.name: Al-Shabaab (Harakat al-Shabaab al-Mujahideen)
armed_groups.geography: Dominant in South Central Somalia (Jubaland, South West State, Bay, Bakool, Hiraan, Middle and Lower Shabelle). Active in all regions. As of July 2025, operating within 50km of Mogadishu. In South West State: controls four full districts (one-third of territory), influences rural areas and main roads Mogadishu–Baidoa–Luuq and Mogadishu–Afgoye–Baraawe.
armed_groups.status: Designated terrorist organization (US, UN, EU, AU). Estimated 7,000–12,000 fighters (true strength unknown). Revenue: $100–180 million annually through 'taxtortion.' Organizational structure: stable core, ideologically focused Amniyaat intelligence wing, and fluctuating membership/supporter periphery. As of mid-2025: has reversed most 2022–2023 government gains in Middle Shabelle, formed strategic triangle within 50km of Mogadishu, recaptured Adan Yabaal, Sabiid, Anole, Moqokori, Tardo, Mahaas, and Harardhere (Mudug coast).
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Al-Shabaab operates a Humanitarian Coordination Office (HCO) in areas under its control. The HCO monitors, regulates, registers, and taxes NGOs. Organizations that maintain structured dialogue at all levels — from senior leadership shura to local commanders — have the best track record of sustained access. Single-level engagement is insufficient. Counter-terrorism financing laws (US, EU) create serious legal risk for direct financial transactions; legal counsel must be engaged before operations in AS-controlled areas. Al-Shabaab's propaganda is increasingly sophisticated — attacks near Mogadishu serve as much to undermine government legitimacy as to achieve tactical objectives. The group deliberately timed its 2025 counteroffensive to coincide with the post-ATMIS transition gap.
armed_groups.risks: Al-Shabaab has expelled numerous NGOs, including major UN agencies during the 2011 famine. Expulsion is typically triggered by: perceived government/military affiliation, public criticism, failure to pay registration fees, or operating without HCO clearance. Staff detention and targeted killings of perceived informants have occurred. Al-Shabaab's Amniyaat intelligence wing is highly sophisticated — staff identities and organizational affiliations are often known before field teams arrive. Enforcement of its tax system: documented repercussions for non-payment include abduction of clan elders (15 abducted near Afmadow July 2023), targeted killings (Bosaso businessman, Feb 2024), and threats to financial institutions. Al-Shabaab also recruits children forcibly: over 900 children recruited and 663 abducted in 2022 alone, primarily from Bay, Bakool, Hiraan, and Lower/Middle Shabelle.

armed_groups.name: Somali National Army (SNA)
armed_groups.geography: Nationwide, primary operations in South Central Somalia
armed_groups.status: Primary CT/stabilization partner. Approximately 32,000 on paper, ~19,000 deployable. Force generation model is structurally fragmented: troops recruited, trained, and equipped through separate bilateral programs (US-trained Danab, Turkish-trained Gorgor/Haramcad, EU-trained general forces, UK-trained logistics/support), resulting in loosely integrated forces with limited central command and poor interoperability. Defense budget: ~$113 million annually, 83% consumed by salaries. 'Ghost soldier' payroll fraud is significant and affects unit cohesion. SNA's SSDP targets: 30,000 land forces, 40,000 police, 8,500 custodial corps.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Do not assume SNA units share command structure, operational doctrine, or even chain of command. The Danab and Gorgor are special forces strike units — they are not designed for hold-phase stabilization and their overuse in that role creates serious operational gaps. Unit commanders are frequently reshuffled, disrupting stability and training continuity. Clan composition of SNA units varies by region and directly affects community perception. Turkish-trained Gorgor units have been documented deployed against domestic political opponents (fired on protesters, arrested journalists), which affects their operational legitimacy in some areas.
armed_groups.risks: Being seen as embedded with or funded by SNA operations will compromise access in al-Shabaab areas and reduce community trust in CVE programs. Minimal civil-military coordination exists at working level. SNA forces with as little as 3 months training have been placed in security roles previously held by AU forces — do not assume professional military standards in post-clearance areas.

armed_groups.name: AUSSOM (African Union Support and Stabilization Mission in Somalia)
armed_groups.geography: Four sectors. 14 main operating bases. Major locations: Mogadishu, Kismaayo, Baidoa, Beledweyne.
armed_groups.status: Replaced ATMIS January 1, 2025. Authorized: 11,911 personnel (11,146 military, 680 police, 85 civilians). Troop contributors: Uganda (4,500), Ethiopia (2,500), Djibouti (1,520), Kenya (1,410), Egypt (1,091). Burundi opted out. TCCs called for 8,000 additional personnel in April 2025 summit. Facing severe funding crisis: less than 20% of Jan–June 2025 costs mobilized. AUSSOM CONOPS outlines a five-year phased mission (Phase 1: Jan–Jul 2025, Phases 2a–2c: Jul 2025–Dec 2027, Phase 3 transition: 2028, Phase 4 exit: 2029). Exit is linked to SSDP implementation progress, not fixed timelines. Target: 80% reduction in al-Shabaab activity. Uganda withdrew forces after suicide vehicle bomb attacks in July 2025 killed 30+ troops.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Coordinate through UN CMCoord mechanisms; avoid direct operational embedding. Egypt's inclusion as TCC reflects Egypt–Ethiopia geopolitical rivalry, not purely Somalia stabilization priorities. AUSSOM's stated mandate includes civilian protection and humanitarian facilitation, but civil-military coordination with humanitarian actors at working level remains weak. The Comprehensive Approach to Security (CAS) framework exists but coordination is incomplete.
armed_groups.risks: AUSSOM funding crisis is the dominant operational risk: if the mission loses capability, security vacuums will expand rapidly. The ATMIS–AUSSOM drawdown pattern is documented: AU force reductions were not replaced by Somali forces as planned, and al-Shabaab exploited these gaps. This pattern is continuing. Do not assume AUSSOM area coverage is stable; reassess continuously.

armed_groups.name: Islamic State-Somalia (ISS)
armed_groups.type: Jihadist group / global Islamic State affiliate
armed_groups.geography: Primary: Bari region of Puntland (Al-Miskaad mountains and cave systems). Minimal presence in South Central Somalia. Not a significant operational factor outside Puntland.
armed_groups.status: Designated terrorist organization. Estimated 600–1,600 fighters; grew from ~500 in 2024, approximately 60% are foreign nationals (Yemen, Tanzania, Libya, Algeria, Tunisia, Sudan, Ethiopia, Syria). IS-Somalia's al-Karrar coordination office in Puntland reportedly directs global IS financing, confirmed by AFRICOM General Langley in 2025. Has deployed drone attacks against Puntland security forces (from January 2025). Puntland's Operation Hilaac (launched January 2025) mobilized ~3,000 forces; had early success pushing IS-Somalia from key supply routes.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Do not conflate IS-Somalia with al-Shabaab — they are rival organizations with different geographies, command structures, and access protocols. US conducted 22 strikes against IS-Somalia through June 10, 2025 (vs 16 against al-Shabaab). US and UAE are conducting airstrikes in Puntland in coordination with Puntland authorities, not the FGS. IS-Somalia has attracted significant AFRICOM attention due to its global homeland security threat framing.
armed_groups.risks: Dual-threat environment creates resource competition: if Puntland deploys south to fight al-Shabaab (as signaled April 2025), Operation Hilaac weakens. The IS-Somalia foreign fighter presence (60% non-Somali) creates distinct engagement risks from al-Shabaab operations.

armed_groups.name: Clan Militias / Local Armed Groups (including Ma'awisley)
armed_groups.geography: Nationwide — Hawiye, Darod, Dir, Rahanweyn, and subclan variants. Ma'awisley most active in Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle.
armed_groups.status: Variable alignment — some allied with FGS, some with al-Shabaab, some independent. Ma'awisley clan militia self-mobilization — particularly Haber Gedir/Saleeban sub-clan of Hawiye — was the most consequential factor in early 2022 SNA gains. This momentum was NOT replicated in Galmudug due to hasty offensive expansion without prior clan engagement. Hawadle clan militias in Hiiraan were reportedly fighting al-Shabaab alone by May 2025 due to decline in federal support.
armed_groups.engagement_notes: Clan structures are the primary social organizing unit in Somalia. No CVE, reintegration, or stabilization program succeeds without clan elder buy-in. Clan elders are also the frontline of CVE — parents bring radicalized children to them first, and most defections from al-Shabaab occur through clan-brokered informal channels, not formal government programs. The Ma'awisley model demonstrates that clan militia fighting alongside national forces against al-Shabaab can be decisive — but requires sustained political and material support.
armed_groups.risks: Engaging one clan's network without equivalent engagement with others creates partisan perception and security risk. Clan militia support is fragile: requires ongoing maintenance, material support, and visible FGS commitment. 145 police officers 'disappeared' during al-Shabaab's 2025 offensive, some suspected of defecting — suggesting security forces with weak clan integration may be unreliable under pressure.

lessons: CVE interventions must be geographically differentiated: programs appropriate for FGS-controlled areas are counterproductive in al-Shabaab permanent-control areas. Map territorial control before designing any CVE activity.

Economic incentives are the dominant driver of al-Shabaab recruitment — not ideology. Research shows monthly payments ($60/month to fighter families), material goods, and status are overwhelmingly powerful factors. CVE programs that focus on counter-narrative without addressing economic alternatives are addressing the wrong driver.

Target vulnerable micro-groups, not whole communities. Drivers of radicalization — unemployment, clan grievance, ideology, coercion, family ties — vary significantly by individual and location. Catchall community programs are ineffective.

Government legitimacy is the central CVE variable. People comply with al-Shabaab primarily due to distrust of the FGS, not ideology. Al-Shabaab fills governance gaps the state leaves vacant: courts, taxation, dispute resolution, service provision, and enforcement. CVE interventions that cannot offer credible alternatives have limited impact.

Al-Shabaab's organizational resilience comes from three nested components: a stable core, an ideologically committed Amniyaat directorate, and a fluctuating membership/supporter periphery. Counterterrorism approaches targeting only senior leadership miss the cushioning effect of the broader support structure.

Child recruitment is a major and distinct CVE challenge: over 900 children were recruited and 663 abducted by al-Shabaab in 2022 alone, primarily from Bay, Bakool, Hiraan, Middle Juba, and Lower Shabelle. Child-specific prevention and reintegration programming is not optional — it requires dedicated resources and child protection SOP integration.

Religious counter-narrative is an underused CVE tool: engaging Islamic scholars to issue fatwas against al-Shabaab and creating credible counter-narrative from within the faith tradition has documented effectiveness. Al-Shabaab's ideological claims are vulnerable to Islamic scholarly critique.

The FGS National Strategy and Action Plan for P/CVE (2016) identifies core pillars: military/security operations; community resilience; strategic communications; and addressing root causes (poverty, marginalization, lack of opportunity). External programming should align with this framework rather than creating parallel structures.
Women are critical but underutilized CVE actors. Female community members have insight into family-level radicalization pathways and early warning signals that male-dominated programming misses.

The reversal of military gains sends a powerful counter-CVE signal — it demonstrates that FGS security provision is impermanent. Communities that stood with the government during the offensive now face al-Shabaab retribution. Stabilization programs must build community resilience that survives territorial fluctuations.

The 'clear' phase has repeatedly succeeded; the 'hold' and 'build' phases have consistently failed. The core gap is civil-military coordination between SNA/AUSSOM and civilian stabilization actors in the post-clearance window. Resource constraints matter, but the lack of pre- and post-operation coordination between military and civilian authorities is an equal or greater constraint.

Over-extension is the single most repeated operational error: attempting to liberate more territory before consolidating existing gains has consistently produced setbacks. The 2022–2024 offensive seized over 215 locations and ~65,000 km², but stalled because consolidation never kept pace with advance. Consolidation must precede expansion.

The 0–90 day window after military clearance is the most critical stabilization window. Somalia's National Stabilization Strategy calls for 'early recovery' (food, basic services, initial reconciliation dialogues) in this period. In practice, few civilian activities occur in this window because civilian agencies have not been pre-briefed or pre-positioned.

The military has a legal obligation under IHL (4th Geneva Convention) to maintain responsibility for civilian populations until civil authority is formally transferred. Transfer should happen as quickly as possible — military forces lack competence in governance functions and will rapidly lose legitimacy if seen as long-term administrators. Risk of 'militarizing the humanitarian space' is real.

'Deployable capabilities' must be pre-built: MOILGR and MOIS have identified the need for rapid-deployment packages of police, administrators, and judges to provide temporary governance and rule of law immediately after clearance. Without pre-positioned civilian capacity, cleared areas remain ungoverned and are quickly reclaimed.

Pre-operation coordination is not just best practice — it is operationally necessary: civilian agencies need significantly longer lead times than military operations allow. Bring civilian counterparts into planning early, despite operational security constraints. The current coordination structure (quarterly ministerial meetings with no working-level technical exchanges) is structurally inadequate.

Different security thresholds: military considers an area 'clear' when it can operate there. Civil administration requires security stable enough for civilian life, commerce, and service provision — a higher bar. Build this explicitly into post-clearance handover criteria.

Clan mobilization (Ma'awisley) was the most consequential factor in early 2022 gains in Hiiraan and Middle Shabelle. When the offensive expanded to Galmudug without first securing clan militia engagement, results were significantly worse. Special forces (Danab, Gorgor) were overextended into hold roles they were not designed for — a dedicated hold-force concept is essential.

Al-Shabaab deliberately waited until after ATMIS drawdown was complete before launching its 2025 counteroffensive in Middle Shabelle. This is a documented pattern: al-Shabaab exploits mission transition gaps. Factor any AUSSOM/SNA transition into security planning with maximum risk alerts.

Interclan reconciliation dialogues in stabilized areas show measurable impact when conducted before civil infrastructure repair, not after. Political reconciliation first, physical reconstruction second.

The SNA's clan composition in a given operational area directly affects community perception of post-clearance security. Mismatched clan composition of holding forces is a known driver of al-Shabaab return.

Organizations that succeeded in maintaining access in al-Shabaab areas engaged comprehensively at all levels — senior shura leadership, mid-level commanders, and local fighters. Engagement at only one level is insufficient and often counterproductive.

Al-Shabaab's Humanitarian Coordination Office (HCO) must be engaged as a formal access negotiation counterpart in areas under AS control. Attempting to operate without HCO registration greatly increases expulsion risk.

Counter-terrorism financing laws (US, EU, UN) technically prohibit transactions with al-Shabaab, including registration fees or taxation. This creates an irresolvable legal dilemma for many international NGOs. Resolve your organization's legal position — in writing, with legal counsel — before operations. Do not leave field staff to navigate this without guidance.

Al-Shabaab enforces its tax system with documented violence: 15 clan elders abducted near Afmadow in July 2023 for defying tax collection; a Bosaso businessman killed in February 2024 in a tax dispute; financial institutions threatened in August 2024 for complying with government directives. Do not assume an NGO's neutrality exempts it from these enforcement dynamics.

The culture of silence around AS engagement (driven by CT law risk) reduces collective learning. Where possible, coordinate discreetly with peer agencies on access approaches to avoid reinventing access agreements.

Al-Shabaab has expelled organizations that publicly criticized them, reported on abuses, or were perceived as information-gathering for military actors. Maintain strict operational security on access negotiations.

Principled humanitarian engagement (Humanity, Neutrality, Impartiality, Independence) is a recognized framework that al-Shabaab's HCO has shown some respect for — invoking these principles explicitly in access negotiations has documented value.

Formal defection numbers are far lower than claimed: between October 2021 and August 2022, only 29 actual formal defections were officially recorded despite government claims of over 600 'operatives neutralized or defected.' Most disengagement happens through informal clan-brokered channels, not the formal DRP.

Leaving al-Shabaab is extremely dangerous. Average consideration period before attempting to leave is 6+ months; some take up to 4 years. Al-Shabaab propaganda claims defectors will be tortured by NISA — and documented NISA abuses (see Human Rights category) make this credible to potential defectors. Programs must address the fear of authorities, not just the fear of al-Shabaab.

The DRP formal pathway: risk classification within 72 hours as high-risk (commanders, bomb-makers, those involved in killings — face military courts and potential death sentences) or low-risk (logistics, preachers, fundraisers — access rehabilitation). Centers: 3 male facilities (Mogadishu, Baidoa, Kismayo), 2 female (Baidoa, Kismayo). Combined average enrollment: 500+. Between January–July 2022, approximately 187 men and 186 women completed rehabilitation and reintegration.

Somalia lacks a finalized amnesty law with clear eligibility criteria, procedural timelines, and explicit entitlements. Presidential amnesty declarations are informal and unclarified. This creates false expectations and deters exit. Advocating for legislative clarity is a precondition for DRP effectiveness.

Community acceptance is the single biggest bottleneck for successful reintegration. Ex-combatants who return without prior reconciliation work face rejection, which significantly increases re-recruitment risk. Both the returnee and the receiving community require preparation — not just the returnee.

Clan elder involvement is not optional — it is the mechanism. Most defections occur through informal clan channels triggered by civilian casualties affecting the defector's own clan. Support clan-brokered informal reintegration in parallel with formal DRP programming.

Gender gaps are significant: women who leave al-Shabaab face compounded stigma (combatant status and 'dishonored') and require separate, gender-sensitive pathways. Screening inconsistency for women is documented. As of early 2022, no women had been classified 'high-risk' despite varied roles.

Children exiting al-Shabaab must be treated as victims of recruitment, not security threats. Standard operating procedures for children must be applied rigorously — failure to do so creates protection risks and undermines program legitimacy.

Rehabilitation centers are 'extremely costly, posing serious sustainability problems, while showing little evidence of effectiveness at degrading al-Shabaab' (EUAA, 2023). Transitioning from center-based to community-based technical and vocational training increases reintegration success and reduces costs and security risks.

Economic re-recruitment risk is high: al-Shabaab paid fighters approximately $60/month to families. Micro-enterprise grants address only one of the return drivers. Programs without security stabilization components in the operating area have high recidivism rates.

The 2023 lifting of the UN arms embargo on Somalia's Federal Government created an important context for DDR: weapons accountability frameworks are now expected as part of the compliance conditions. Weapons and Ammunition Management (WAM) programming should be integrated with DDR, not run separately.

Al-Shabaab's annual revenue exceeds $150 million (EUAA 2025), derived from an extremely diversified 'taxtortion' system: checkpoint transit fees, agricultural produce and livestock levies, port import/export taxes (Mogadishu port handles 7,000–10,000 containers monthly), forced 'zakat' payments (2.5% annual levy on wealthy individuals and businesses), kidnapping ransoms, operating license fees, property transaction taxes, and (from 2024) scrap metal levies. The breadth and diversification of this system is the primary challenge for CTF.

Al-Shabaab generates approximately one-third of its revenue from Mogadishu specifically. Its urban financial architecture is sophisticated: the Amniyaat intelligence wing has infiltrated notaries (who provide property transaction data), port officials (who provide cargo manifests), government employees (paid 5% commissions for reporting land sales), and business intermediaries. Financial disruption without addressing this infiltration is ineffective.

Two revenue extraction models operate in Mogadishu: 'Willing' arrangements (businesses receiving al-Shabaab capital loans in exchange for 20–50% of profits) and 'Unwilling' arrangements (al-Shabaab forcing capital into struggling businesses and extracting 80–90% of profits indefinitely). The result is partial ownership of much of Mogadishu's commercial sector.

Flow disruption alone does not work: al-Shabaab consistently adapts by diversifying income when specific revenue streams are targeted. The January 2023 freeze of 250 bank accounts and 70 mobile money accounts — initially claimed to have frozen '$150 million' — actually resulted in only approximately $750,000 frozen. Targeting specific flows without addressing the enabling governance environment produces no lasting effect.

Use a layered disruption model (UNODC): Layer 1 (flow disruption — specific commodities, checkpoints, port revenues, individual designations) must be combined with Layer 2 (systems disruption — governance gaps, corruption, and the parallel authority structure that enables al-Shabaab to extract revenue). Both layers must operate simultaneously.

Somalia's AML/CFT framework has critical structural weaknesses: mobile money providers have been consistently non-compliant; Designated Non-Financial Businesses and Professions (notaries, real estate agents, lawyers) lack awareness of AML/CFT obligations; Somalia's Financial Reporting Centre received only two new AML/CFT case referrals since April 2023; and the national biometric ID system had achieved only 50,000 of a 10 million registration target by April 2025.

The Haleel Group network (Dubai-based, designated by US Treasury OFAC in March 2024) spans Somalia, Kenya, Uganda, Cyprus, and the UAE — demonstrating that al-Shabaab's financial architecture operates through legitimate international business entities. International sanctions coordination through the UN Al-Shabaab Sanctions Committee (Resolution 2713) is the appropriate multilateral mechanism.

Mobile money and hawala networks are major risk vectors that have been systematically underassessed. Al-Shabaab increasingly operates through in-kind taxation (livestock, goods) and informal transfer systems that evade financial monitoring. Building capacity at the Financial Reporting Centre, NISA financial intelligence unit, Attorney General's Office, and judiciary is a priority.

For field operators: be aware that normal commercial transactions in al-Shabaab-influenced areas may involve al-Shabaab revenue extraction at multiple points in the supply chain. Procurement, logistics, and cash transfer processes in these areas require specific CTF risk assessment and legal guidance before implementation.

Somalia's official SSR goal — security forces that are 'able, affordable, accountable, and acceptable' — is the organizing framework. In practice, 'affordable' and 'accountable' have been consistently underprioritized relative to 'able.' The result is a force whose operational capacity cannot be sustainably financed and whose accountability failures drive al-Shabaab recruitment.

The SNA force generation model produces 'two armies': mobile, well-equipped special forces (Danab, Gorgor) capable of offensive operations, and largely stationary, poorly equipped regular forces. The special forces are strike units not designed for hold-phase stabilization — their overuse in that role is a documented operational failure and creates serious hold-phase gaps.

SSR is fundamentally a political process, not a technical one (UN CROSSROADS 2024). The primary obstacle to integrating Federal Member State forces into the SNA under the 2017 National Security Architecture framework is clan-based political resistance from FMS leaders, not technical capacity gaps. Political incentives for FMS leaders to contribute forces must be structured into the process.

The SNA's 2023 defense budget: ~$113 million, 83% consumed by salaries. Somalia cannot independently sustain its current force structure — two-thirds of FGS revenue comes from external donors. 'Ghost soldier' payroll fraud is a priority accountability issue that directly affects unit cohesion and operational readiness. Financial accountability and soldier pay reliability are security investments, not administrative details.

Irregular police pay is a primary driver of police predation on civilians — which directly reinforces al-Shabaab's anti-government narrative. Paying police regularly and on time is a CVE measure, not only a governance one.

Turkish-trained Gorgor and Haramcad units have been documented deployed against domestic political opponents, including firing on protesters and arresting journalists. This politicization of security forces undermines their operational legitimacy in affected communities and provides al-Shabaab with recruitment narratives.

The SSDP (Security Sector Development Plan) targets 30,000 land forces, 40,000 police, and 8,500 custodial corps members. But achieving these numbers means nothing without unified command structure, interoperable training, and integrated logistical support. These structural elements are currently absent.

Building institutional oversight capacity (human rights monitoring, parliamentary accountability, anti-corruption mechanisms) alongside operational capacity is essential. Donors frequently prioritize military outputs at the expense of governance, which weakens accountability and generates civilian grievances that benefit al-Shabaab. The Anti-Corruption Commission was dissolved in 2022 — there is currently no functional independent oversight architecture.

Stabilization force concept: while SNA units can execute offensive operations, building dedicated 'hold' forces capable of delivering governance and stability to recovered settlements remains critically underdeveloped. This is the most urgent structural gap in Somalia's SSR.

CT abuses are al-Shabaab's most effective recruitment tool. Documented NISA practices — arbitrary detention without warrants, months without charge, torture allegations (sometimes resulting in death), and detention in informal 'safe houses' — are actively cited by al-Shabaab in its propaganda and credibly deter potential defectors from engaging with formal pathways. Accountability is not just a human rights obligation: it is operationally necessary.

NISA members cannot be investigated or prosecuted without permission of NISA's Director-General, who also enjoys personal immunity. This structural immunity provision prevents accountability for documented violations. Field operators should not assume that FGS CT partners are operating within legal frameworks that permit independent oversight.

Civilian terrorism suspects in Somalia are prosecuted in military courts rather than civilian courts. Between 2017 and 2021, military courts tried 659 alleged terrorists with 455 convictions and 17 executions. Absence of civilian court jurisdiction raises serious fair trial concerns that affect the legitimacy of CT prosecutions and create grievances.

Somalia has been without functional independent oversight institutions for years: no National Human Rights Commission, no Ombudsman, no Constitutional Court, no functioning Anti-Corruption Commission (dissolved 2022). Field operators cannot rely on institutional accountability channels when documenting security force violations.

Military operations have caused significant civilian casualties: approximately one-third of civilian casualties between 2017 and 2023 were caused by state forces and militias. A March 2024 airstrike killed 21 civilians with no independent investigation conducted. Civilian casualty tracking and investigation capacity within the armed forces is absent.

Puntland executed four individuals in 2024 who were children when they committed their alleged offenses — violating international law on juvenile death penalties. Child protection standards must be explicitly addressed when working with Puntland security forces on IS-Somalia operations.

For international humanitarian and CVE operators: UN Security Council Resolution language should explicitly exclude independent humanitarian assistance from counter-terrorism sanctions application. Organizations operating in al-Shabaab-affected areas face a legal environment where CT law can be weaponized against neutral humanitarian actors. Know your legal protections before deploying.

Human rights documentation by field operators — even if unofficial — creates the evidence base for eventual accountability and for access negotiations with armed actors. Document incidents systematically, protect sources, and route information through appropriate channels (OHCHR, OCHA protection cluster).

Political infighting between FGS and Federal Member States is the primary driver of stalled CT and stabilization operations. The NCC (National Consultative Council) has failed to function as a genuine consensus-building mechanism. Puntland has boycotted NCC since January 2023. Jubaland-FGS tensions led to armed confrontation at Ras Kamboni in late 2024, resulting in several hundred federal forces withdrawing to Kenya after military defeat.

The FGS constitutional amendment process (approved March 2024) — which expanded presidential powers, abolished the PM position, introduced universal suffrage, and extended presidential terms — has deepened political divisions. Critics (including former presidents Farmaajo and Sharif Sheikh Ahmed, and two former PMs) view it as an illegitimate centralization of power. Track this political track as a key risk variable in stabilization assessments.

The 4.5 power-sharing formula (distributing political representation among Hawiye, Darood, Digil-Mirifle, Dir, and minority coalition) structures all Somali political decision-making. The clan identity of your interlocutor shapes their political incentives. No program that ignores this structure will achieve durable results.

Clan consensus is as critical as formal political agreement. President Mohamud struggled to rally support from his own Abgaal sub-clan (Hawiye) during the 2025 al-Shabaab counteroffensive. Any program that lacks buy-in at multiple clan levels — not just at FGS level — is exposed to rapid reversal when political conditions shift.

Federal-FMS political tensions create direct security risks: al-Shabaab exploited the Jubaland–FGS armed standoff to attack Jubaland forces at Bulo Haji while both sides were distracted. Political disputes between Somali actors create operational windows for al-Shabaab. Monitor FGS-FMS relations as a leading indicator of security risk.

FMS (Jubaland, South West State, Hirshabelle, Galmudug, SSC-Khaatumo) have independent security and political relationships with al-Shabaab. Federal government coordination does not automatically transfer to member state operations. Always identify whether you are operating under FGS or FMS authority and map the relevant political dynamics separately.

External pressure for national dialogue has limited effect without inclusive process: the May 2025 NCC meeting proceeded without Puntland and Jubaland, drew US and international criticism, and produced outcomes that further inflamed tensions. Inclusive process is a prerequisite for NCC legitimacy and CT coordination effectiveness.

lessons.category: AUSSOM, External Actors, and Mission Transition
lessons: AUSSOM faces a structural funding crisis: less than 20% of its January–June 2025 costs were mobilized. ATMIS left outstanding TCC liabilities of ~$93.9 million. The US is blocking UNSCR 2719 assessed contributions. The EU has been cutting annual contributions. Plan all operations assuming AUSSOM capabilities may degrade significantly.

The five-year AUSSOM CONOPS (2025–2029) links mission exit to SSDP implementation progress, not fixed timelines. This is operationally important: stabilization program design should align with AUSSOM's phase benchmarks (securing key locations 2025–2027, transition 2028, exit 2029), recognizing these timelines are aspirational given current funding gaps.

The ATMIS-to-AUSSOM transition is a case study in transition gap exploitation: AU drawdowns created security vacuums that Somali forces could not fill, and al-Shabaab launched its 2025 counteroffensive precisely during the transition window. Uganda withdrew forces after July 2025 VBIED attacks. This pattern will repeat unless force generation and funding are specifically addressed.

External actors engage Somalia bilaterally in ways that fragment command: US (Danab), Turkey (Gorgor/Haramcad), UAE (various bilateral), Egypt (defense pact August 2024), EU (EUTM), Eritrea and Uganda (separate training programs). The result is multiple SNA factions with incompatible training, equipment, and command affiliations. This is not a coordination problem — it is a structural feature of the security architecture.

Egypt's entry into Somalia's security architecture (defense pact August 2024, arms deliveries, AUSSOM troops) is primarily driven by Egypt–Ethiopia geopolitical competition, not Somalia stabilization priorities. Egypt is using Somalia as leverage against Ethiopia over Nile waters and Red Sea access. Factor this into assessments of AUSSOM internal cohesion.

US airstrike escalation in 2025 (38 strikes through June 10, nearly 4x the 2024 total) is increasingly framed around US homeland security justifications, not Somalia regional stability. This framing affects target prioritization (IS-Somalia getting 22 of 38 strikes vs 16 for al-Shabaab) in ways that may not align with Somalia's primary internal security needs.

Turkey and the US have provided consistent drone and airstrike support to FGS operations. However, airstrikes alone cannot substitute for effective hold-phase operations — this is a documented lesson from every phase of the conflict. US and Turkish CT support amplifies offensive capacity without addressing the stabilization gap.

Advocate for predictable multi-year AUSSOM financing as a core advocacy objective. The lesson from AMISOM and ATMIS is unambiguous: ad hoc donor pledges and delayed contributions directly compromise mission effectiveness and create the security gaps al-Shabaab exploits.

lessons.category: Engagement with Government and Coordination Architecture
lessons: NISA (National Intelligence and Security Agency) is the primary CT coordination counterpart for information sharing. NISA relationships are valuable but carry the same neutrality risk as SNA association — and NISA's documented human rights record (arbitrary detention, torture allegations, structural immunity from prosecution) creates additional reputational and legal risk for partner organizations.

The UN Integrated Task Force (ITF) coordinates UN political, security, and humanitarian actors in Somalia. Participate in ITF and CMCoord mechanisms to avoid duplicating access negotiations and to benefit from collective learning.
Track OCHA Somalia's quarterly Humanitarian Access Overview — it provides the most current public assessment of access constraints by zone and is the reference document for access planning.

Donors and external partners widely agree the conflict cannot be resolved through military means alone. Political agreement between FGS, FMS, and key stakeholders — finalizing the constitution through inclusive process, agreeing on national security architecture, and coordinating federal/state/clan forces — is a necessary condition for lasting stabilization. This political track is as important as the military track for field program viability.

Rebalance assistance toward sub-national actors: directing most assistance through the FGS reinforces a power imbalance with FMS that does not reflect actual institutional capacity and creates political tensions that al-Shabaab exploits. Diversifying support to FMS administrations and local authorities strengthens the overall governance ecosystem.

Monitoring and evaluation in non-permissive environments requires specific investment: standard USAID/donor portfolio management tools are systematically underused in Somalia, resulting in poor indicator consistency and limited ability to course-correct. Build third-party monitoring with standardized processes and tracked corrective action timelines into program design from the start.
